title: THE FOUR OLDS — cold open table read

lines.text: [calm, setting the scene] Election night. A wall-sized map, most of it undecided gray. Confetti cannons stand by, unfired. The board flips a state blue. Then another. A production assistant claps twice, alone, then stops.

[professional, tightening with disbelief] At eleven-oh-four eastern, we can now project — Zoran Marwani, the junior senator from New York, the first D-S-A affiliated nominee ever to carry a major party — has been elected President of the United States.

The studio erupts. Balloons fall on the news desk. Someone has printed HISTORY IS HEALING on a bedsheet in the crowd outside the window. The commentator, silver-haired, removes his glasses. His eyes are wet on camera.
[moved, voice unsteady] Forty-one years I've covered this building. Perhaps tonight — [swallows] perhaps tonight begins the reconciliation with a world the last administration spent years insulting.
[dry] The chyron below him rolls: markets, cautious. Allies, jubilant.

Two months later. Inauguration crowd to the horizon. On the jumbotron, President Marwani, forty-one, hand off the Bible, already at the podium. Flags on the stage: the fifty stars — and beside it, same height, the U-N blue.
[measured, presidential, unhurried] I do not stand here to celebrate a victory. I stand here to begin a repair. On behalf of the United States — to every nation we have wronged — [beat] we are sorry.
The crowd roars. The roar rolls down the Mall in a wave.
[rising, emphatic] This is not retreat. This is not surrender. This is repair.
[flat] Under the podium feed, the chyron: Nobel Committee awards Peace Prize, citing, quote, the announced agenda.

[low] Cold metal light. Pallets nose to tail, shrink-wrapped. Under the plastic: banded bricks of hundred-dollar bills. A loadmaster walks the line with a clipboard manifest. Stamped across the top page: Iran Compensation Framework. Disbursement one of twelve.
[deadpan] At the ramp, a young State Department aide turns his back to the pallets, raises his phone, and takes a selfie. He checks it. Takes a better one.
Chandeliers. A long table of ministers. The U-S Treasury Secretary signs a document the size of a phone book; a European minister pats his shoulder twice. Champagne comes out on trays.
[dry] The chyron on the broadcast feed: United States commits two hundred billion dollars to European defense renewal. Below it, a stock ticker. All red.

A push alert stacks over a family photo: White House unveils Stewardship Act. Private assets above one billion to be administered in the public interest. [beat] A thumb swipes it away. Another alert lands on top of it.
Packed gallery. Magnus Hale alone at the witness table, reading from a single prepared page. His voice is level and dead.
[flat, toneless, reading a statement that is not his] Frontier is proud to serve the goals of this administration. At home, and beyond it.

[savoring every word, slow] Proud. That's good. Because some of my colleagues feel a capability this essential shouldn't rest in private hands at all. There's a word for the remedy. I want you to hear me say it slowly. [pause] Nationalization.
The Senator leans back. Cameras click. [beat] Hale folds his page in half. And in half again. And says nothing.

[hushed] A counter town diner. Six regulars, coffee, forks down. Nobody talks. On the wall TV, muted: the Brussels champagne, the falling balloons, the jumbotron apology, cycling. Earlene refills Buck's cup. Buck, seventies, feed cap, doesn't look up from the counter.

[distant, faint, bureaucratic] ...effective January first, the private sale and discharge of consumer fireworks is prohibited nationwide, under the administration's binding carbon framework. Municipal displays may apply for cultural exemption through the...
[quiet] Nobody moves to turn it up. The percolator finishes. Buck turns his cup a quarter turn on the saucer.
[a long beat, then flat, almost to himself] Huh.
[quiet] He drinks.
